title: 2026 02 19 EDSI 1
displayTitle: 2026-02-19 - Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee - Commerce (Promoting Competition and Other Matters) Amendment Bill
committee: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee
topic: Commerce (Promoting Competition and Other Matters) Amendment Bill
description: Commerce (Promoting Competition and Other Matters) Amendment Bill New Zealand Law Society Michael Tilley, Commercial and Business Law Committee member Victoria Fowler, Commercial and Business Law Committee member Civil Contractors New Zealand Alan Pollard, Chief Executive Fraser May, Communications and Advocacy Manager
descriptionSummary: Commerce (Promoting Competition and Other Matters) Amendment Bill. New Zealand Law Society. member

chunks.text: Thank you, Victoria. Are we waiting for Michael as well? Oh, yes. I can see he is coming online now. Yeah. Thank you so much for your submission. We have 10 minutes with you. Thanks. Thank you. And thank you for the time to speak with you this afternoon on behalf of the New Zealand Law Society. Um, I'm Victoria Fowler. I'm counsel at Web Henderson, uh, with a practice focusing on, um, regulatory litigation and advice on competition and consumer issues, um, with a particular focus on matters regulated by the Commerce Commission.

So appreciate the opportunity to speak with you about these important changes to the Commerce Act. Um, appearing with Michael Tilly, who is a competition lawyer at Til and Co. And, um, former Chief Legal Counsel Competition and head of Mergers at the Commerce Commission. Um, we are both here in our capacity as members of the Law Society More Reform Committee.

Um, as committee members. We, uh, along with others, provided feedback on the bill to the Law Society to assist them in drafting the submission you have for you. Um, so the, the Law Society supports the bill's intention to update and modernize competition settings, um, and subject to some amendments.

We support the continued passage of the bill. Um, in our time today, we'll focus on a, a couple of the, the biggest concerns that we have, um, in order to lose some time for questions. Um, but the, the written submission you have before you goes into detail about some, some changes that the law society considers are required, um, for the, uh, proper functioning of the changes.

Um, the, before I move on to talk about one of the, uh, key par parts that we'd like to discuss today, um, I wanted to touch on a general view on alignment with international, uh, comparators. So we, we generally support greater alignment, particularly with Australian settings, unless there is a good reason to diverge, um, changing New Zealand law in a way that doesn't align with equivalent jurisdictions.

And in particular with Australia, it increases legal uncertainty, and so that can affect investment and competition in New Zealand markets. Uh, so while that is the, the sort of default setting, uh, we do think that there are some circumstances in which it's reasonable to diverge from the position in Australia.

Uh, and one of the parts of the changes that we think falls into that category, um, is the, the changes to the substantially lessening of competition test. Um, so the, the SLC test is intended to ensure that mergers are not going to undermine the competitive market structure of the industry that's affected by a particular merger.

The, the bill proposes to introduce new wording so that the SLC test will, uh, effectively consider a merger, a, a killer acquisition if it creates strengthens or entrenches a substantial degree of power in the market. Uh, so those words were imported into the wording of the Australian SLC test, um, in Australia that is restricted in application to just merger control.

Um, so that's different to the way that the current bill is drafted in New Zealand, which proposes to expand the SLC test across the board, so including in, in restrictive trade practices. Um, so that expands the test further than in Australia. Uh, the, the Law society's recommendation is that no change is made to the SLC test at all.

Um, we consider that divergence from Australia in, in that case is reasonable, primarily because there is minimal evidence of a problem to be solved in New Zealand. Um, that's especially so given the additional powers that are proposed to be granted to the commission, um, that would address any potential risk in a more targeted and responsive way.

So, uh, the, the primary submission, um, is that the, the clause amending the SLC test is deleted in its entirety. Um, but, but if it is to be pursued at very least that it should be narrowed in its application, um, so that, like in Australia, it only applies to mergers. Uh, so, uh, that is the, the submission on that point.

And I'll pass over to Mr. Tilley now to address the next points. Thank you, Victoria. Good afternoon, everybody. Thank you for the opportunity to appear. Um, the next submission is on predatory pricing. So, as with the SLC test changes, the explanatory materials to the bill disclosed little evidence of an issue with predatory pricing in New Zealand that requires amendments to the act.

We think it's also important to note that the cost test proposed for the new prohibition did not increase New Zealand's alignment with comparative jurisdictions as the bill intends. We would also note that section 36 of the act, so the, the prohibition on the misuse of market power, which currently deals with predatory pricing, it's currently, uh, less than three years old in its current form and has therefore not been enforced long enough to determine its effectiveness.

So overall, the proposed change risks damaging business confidence in decision making on pricing. In a way that means that large businesses are likely to respond by pricing things higher to be on the safe side, resulting in negative impacts for consumers. So we would recommend that the predatory pricing changes are not, uh, pursued in the bill.

I'll now move on to the final point on which we would like to submit today, which is the confidential information. So the bill proposes to insert significant restrictions on the operation of the Official Information Act in relation to official information held by the Commerce Commission. We're referring here to clause 53, which proposes new restrictions on the OIA. The OIA is a key mechanism by which government bodies are held accountable for their activities.

It already provides withholding grounds for the provision of confidential information as well as commercially sensitive information as set out in the submission. The policy problem here seems to be initial perception rather than reality in the view of the law society. The more nuanced option provided in the regulatory impact statements, that that alternative procedural and institutional improvements could be pursued, would be preferable.

This could look like providing Commerce Commission staff with additional training and guidance from the ombudsman on the application of the OIA and where and when the withholding provisions can be used for commercially sensitive and confidential information. This would likely translate into improved communication with businesses and individuals about the risks of information they provide being released, as well as improved practice in recognizing when and where the existing withholding provisions of the OIA should apply, leading

to improved confidence in sharing the relevant information with the commission. Those are our submissions. Um, happy to take any questions. Good, uh, questions on this. Um, Dr. David Wilson. Um, this predatory pricing issue just keeps coming up and we are not getting alignment today in terms of preference on what should happen with this.

We had a presentation from the University of Sydney, which says that we are currently in a situation, we have vertical integration, dual conflicts, in other words, competing with your own suppliers, uh, promotional discounts, results, um, exclusive dealing pricing algorithms and so on that take advantage of a whole bunch of things.

So you could say that this is monopolistic behavior, um, and we've had, I think, real problems trying to understand whether or not there is actually predatory pricing already going on. So I think a number of us would think that there is, and a number of the consumers would think that there is.

Um, so you are against, uh, trying to address this in the way that we have. Can you provide us with the way that you believe that there actually is a way forward to, in, you know, give consumers confidence that we are addressing this issue? Um, sure.

I mean, I think that that's a, um, that's a, a, a, a policy, a policy issue and, and we'd be happy to take a look at, at any, any drafting or any alternative drafting. I think our message here today is that, um, the surrounding materials to the bill do not provide strong evidence or do not disclose to us that there is an issue to be, um, to be addressed.
All right. Um, thank you for your submission. Thank you. Uh, next we have civil contractors, New Zealand, again on Zoom.

Excellent. Uh, can you hear us, Madam Chair? Yes. Uh, we can hear you, but we can't see you, but that's totally fine if you Oh, yeah. Uh, I can see freezer now. Um, got it. Thanks. You as well, Alan. Thank you so much. And we have 10 minutes with you. Thank you. Brilliant. Hey, thank you. Thanks, Madam Chair, the opportunity to, to address you. So I'm a Pollard, I'm Chief executive, the civil contractor, New Zealand.

I'm joined online by my colleague Fraser Mo, who's our communications and advocacy manager. Um, or obviously take our submission as Reid will only be, uh, brief. There's really three things that I want to emphasize. The first is, we're really concerned to make sure that we continue to support, um, and allow the positive collaborative model set to exist for project delivery.

So Alliance has joint ventures, panels all used with the client, or one or more other parties worked together to deliver a project early supplier engagement. Also, part of that is used to, uh, to ensure that designs are practical and that can include collaboration between competing firms under a single client as well.

I mean, these models have been proven to add value where the project scope and risks are uncertain, uh, where there's significant time constraints, where it's a technically challenging project where there could be external, uh, complex external factors where innovative solutions are needed. Flexibility is important.

The collective approach helps manage risk, and there's an expectation of knowledge sharing between the parties. Um, they are currently embedded in very large infrastructure projects in New Zealand. They're often codified in the contract. So we support healthy competition on the market, but we are concerned that any changes must ensure that this positive collaboration continue.

And to put that in context, the C committee will have seen the announcement on Tuesday, uh, by the infrastructure commission with a 30 year plan, some very significant project on the 272 70 $5 billion pipeline. Many of those are gonna rely on these collaborative structures, uh, remaining, uh, as acceptable, uh, to the business and legal community.

Uh, it's our submission that, uh, the Regi, the legislation does risk jeopardizing these models in its current form. And we, uh, if that is the case, we would hope the Commerce Commission would make immediate class exemptions, uh, to allow them to continue. Um, secondly, we support efforts, uh, to combat predatory pricing.

We urge the committee and the commission to be mindful though of any unintended consequences. There's many situations where businesses offer discount or lower prices, or they might offer to work at or below cost because of some community. Good. And these are positive, as we pointed in our submission. There may be cases where prices are lowered for the benefit of communities, for example, constructing, uh, walking tracks for public good in partnership, the charitable community groups as it stands, we are concerned that, uh,

the provisions may not allow for this. Uh, we also want the commission to be mindful about the role procurement practices play in determining prices that, uh, eventually paid for contracts. You, you may be aware of the past 18 months, our industry has suffered from a significant lack of work being brought to market, and that has resulted sometimes in projects being tended, um, below cost just to keep teams busy, uh, rather than lay them off.

There are examples where clients have used that market situation to force prices down even further. Um, the third point is we really need to avoid unnecessary regulatory burden, such as the risk of New Zealand religious reform, disadvan disadvantaging, our New Zealand businesses compared to our Australian cousins.

We do lose businesses when that happens. So we know that in Australia conduct creating, strengthening or entrenching a substantial degree of power in the market only relates to acquisitions. But in New Zealand, from what we read, it's intended to apply to all Commerce Act prohibition. So we believe that's too broad and will most likely stifle competition.

Um, can I invite my colleague, uh, to add any comments that he would like to make? Yeah. Okay. Thanks Alan. Um, and, um, greeting committee, uh, I'd just like to comment briefly around a little more detail around re regulatory burden. As, as Alan's already commented, um, we've got some infrastructure affordability issues and, and we believe that some of that does come down to regulatory burden.

So, um, really the points around notifications. So, um, the amount of notification and the confusion they may cause. Um, we're not quite certain what, how, how much of that this legislative form will generate for the industry. And, um, if we're looking to make, make projects more efficient and bring 'em to market, uh, swiftly, um, I mean, for example, I might just give you one of the clauses that require that, that gives the commission 30 working days to respond.

And, um, if they do not respond, then they're deemed to, to have declined to give the clearance. And that's, uh, clause 65 BA and um, four. Um, and if you apply that to collective bargaining with, um, two or more persons who supply or require goods, it's quite a lot of notification and quite a lot of room there for, um, perhaps accidental decline or, or, you know, capacity to deal with that.

So this is an example of where regulatory burden can kind of slow down infrastructure projects. If these kind of class exemptions, I mean, for recognized working models aren't, aren't, um, allowed or looked at in detail and without clear guidance, it's really hard for us to know if that will be, you know, the changes will be successful. I mean, in summary, we, we, we support the provisions and to foster competition, but um, we we're not sure what's proposed.

Um, we'll streamline as intended, and um, you know, we just ask you to consider that. And, um, tha thank you very much for taking the time to hear us and um, we, we'd welcome any questions that you have for us. Any questions, Travis? So a quick question. So, um, what I understand is you're saying that your industry works, um, slightly differently from other industries, uh, given, uh, there has been a reduction in work recently.

When you say work differently, uh, can you elaborate? Mean contracts, availability of contracts? So I don't understand the question you're asking. So, um, so in the, um, because you are, um, when we talk about the infrastructure construction industry, so are you saying that, um, because of a lack of projects, uh, the behavior has changed in the recent months and it operate differently from other industries?

So I if we compare with other industries, so you think this won't happen in other industries and it's specific to, uh, the infrastructure construction industry. Um, o obviously our familiar familiarity is only with construction, but I can say that, uh, vertical and horizontal parts of the construction sector have experienced the same challenges.

So what we've seen, uh, as we mentioned is, uh, if you like some, um, um, low balling if, for want of a better word, of contracted prices to take advantage of the fact that contractors, myself had little or no work. Mm-hmm. If I could just build on that. So in terms of the previous submitter and the point that they made, um, it's often a procurement issue.

So, you know, a lot of this is actually handled through, because we are working on public sector contracts in terms of government contracts or consulate contracts for local government. Um, a lot of the terms are set through those, through the procurement terms of, of government. And um, also a lot of, a lot of the behavior is driven through procurement.

So, uh, perhaps part of our submission is that the solution could set at a procurement level rather than a legislative level. And, um, that may be appropriate. And this case, because a lot of these things are already handled in detail in procurement and in the construction contracts that we work under, um, the, the, the fact of having, uh, dealt with through another piece of legislation adds confusion and, um, reduces certainty for our, for our members who, who already know how, how the system works, um, under, under their, under their tendering

to their, to their clients. Um, so yeah, that, that's, that's possibly a better way, a good way to put that. Um, does that make sense? And that's, that's good. Thank you so much. Any questions, members? No thank you. No, no questions. Thank you so much for your submission. Thank You very much. Thank you. Alright, with that members, we have concluded hearing from submitters for today.
